fragments.content: Cancer screening aims to detect cancer before symptoms develop, when treatment is more effective. Screening criteria (Wilson-Jungner): important health problem, understood natural history, recognizable early stage, effective treatment available, suitable test, acceptable to population, cost-effective. Mammography uses low-dose X-rays to detect breast cancer; digital breast tomosynthesis (3D mammography) improves cancer detection, reduces false positives. Colonoscopy visualizes entire colon, allows polyp removal; alternatives include FIT (fecal immunochemical test), stool DNA (Cologuard), CT colonography. PSA (prostate-specific antigen) blood test for prostate cancer screening is controversial due to overdiagnosis of indolent cancers. Low-dose CT for lung cancer screening in high-risk smokers. Cervical cancer screening: Pap smear (cytology) and HPV testing. Screening intervals and ages vary by guideline organization (USPSTF, ACS, specialty societies).

Screening recommendations based on evidence of mortality reduction. Breast cancer: USPSTF recommends biennial mammography ages 50-74; individualize ages 40-49. ACS recommends annual starting at 45, biennial after 55. High-risk women (BRCA mutation, strong family history) start earlier with MRI + mammography. Colorectal cancer: average risk start age 45 (ACS) or 50 (USPSTF); options include colonoscopy every 10 years, FIT annually, stool DNA every 3 years, CT colonography every 5 years. Continue until age 75; individualize 76-85. Prostate cancer: shared decision-making ages 55-69; discuss risks/benefits including overdiagnosis, overtreatment. High-risk (African American, family history) may start at 40-45. Lung cancer: annual low-dose CT ages 50-80 with ≥20 pack-year smoking history, current smoker or quit <15 years. Cervical cancer: ages 21-29 Pap every 3 years; ages 30-65 Pap+HPV every 5 years or Pap alone every 3 years.

Screening test performance measured by sensitivity (detects true positives), specificity (correctly identifies negatives), positive predictive value (probability cancer present with positive test). Mammography sensitivity 75-85%, lower in dense breasts; specificity ~90%. False positives lead to unnecessary biopsies, anxiety. Overdiagnosis detects cancers that would never cause harm (DCIS, indolent prostate cancers), leading to overtreatment. PSA screening: sensitivity ~80% but specificity only ~60%; many elevated PSAs are benign (BPH, prostatitis). Number needed to screen (NNS) to prevent one death: mammography ~1000 women screened 10 years; colonoscopy ~300-500; PSA controversial (ERSPC trial NNS ~780, PLCO showed no benefit). Lead-time bias makes survival appear longer without actually extending life. Length-time bias favors detection of slow-growing tumors. Randomized trials show mortality reduction for mammography (15-25%), colonoscopy (60-70% for detected cancers), lung CT (20%).
